title: Zero-overhead principle
summary: The zero-overhead principle is a C++ design principle that states:

title: C++ language
summary: The C++ language area is where syntax turns into semantics: declarations introduce entities, expressions produce values and side effects, classes define object structure, templates generalize code, and the object model governs lifetime, storage, and initialization. This hub is the practical entry point into those core rules.

title: Abstract class
summary: Defines an abstract type which cannot be instantiated, but can be used as a base class.

title: Access specifiers
summary: In a member-specification of a class/struct or union, define the accessibility of subsequent members.

title: Argument-dependent lookup
summary: Argument-dependent lookup (ADL), also known as Koenig lookup[[1]](#cite_note-1), is the set of rules for looking up the unqualified function names in function-call expressions, including implicit function calls to overloaded operators. These function names are looked up in the namespaces of their arguments in addition to the scopes and namespaces considered by the usual unqualified name lookup.

title: Aggregate initialization
summary: Initializes an aggregate from an initializer list. It is a form of list-initialization(since C++11).

title: alignas specifier (since C++11)
summary: Specifies the alignment requirement of a type or an object.

title: alignof operator (since C++11)
summary: Queries alignment requirements of a type.

title: Array declaration
summary: Declares an object of array type.

title: The as-if rule
summary: Allows any and all code transformations that do not change the observable behavior of the program.

title: Copy assignment operator
summary: A copy assignment operator is a non-template non-static member function with the name operator= that can be called with an argument of the same class type and copies the content of the argument without mutating the argument.

title: ASCII Chart
summary: The following chart contains all 128 ASCII decimal (dec), octal (oct), hexadecimal (hex) and character (ch) codes.

title: asm declaration
summary: asm-declaration gives the ability to embed assembly language source code within a C++ program. This declaration is conditionally-supported and(since C++11)implementation defined, meaning that it may not be present and, even when provided by the implementation,(since C++11)it does not have a fixed meaning.

title: Attribute specifier sequence (since C++11)
summary: Introduces implementation-defined attributes for types, objects, code, etc.

title: C++ attribute: assume (since C++23)
summary: Specifies that the given expression is assumed to always evaluate to true at a given point in order to allow compiler optimizations based on the information given.

title: C++ attribute: carries_dependency (since C++11)
summary: Indicates that dependency chain in release-consume std::memory_order propagates in and out of the function, which allows the compiler to skip unnecessary memory fence instructions.

title: C++ attribute: deprecated (since C++14)
summary: Indicates that the name or entity declared with this attribute is deprecated, that is, the use is allowed, but discouraged for some reason.

title: C++ attribute: fallthrough (since C++17)
summary: Indicates that the fall through from the previous case label is intentional and should not be diagnosed by a compiler that warns on fallthrough.

title: C++ attribute: indeterminate (since C++26)
summary: Indicates that the variable or function parameter has an indeterminate value if it is not initialized.

title: C++ attribute: likely, unlikely (since C++20)
summary: Allow the compiler to optimize for the case where paths of execution including that statement are more or less likely than any alternative path of execution that does not include such a statement.

title: C++ attribute: maybe_unused (since C++17)
summary: Suppresses warnings on unused entities.

title: C++ attribute: no_unique_address (since C++20)
summary: Allows this data member to be overlapped with other non-static data members or base class subobjects of its class.

title: C++ attribute: nodiscard (since C++17)
summary: If a function declared nodiscard or a function returning an enumeration or class declared nodiscard by value is called from a discarded-value expression other than a cast to void, the compiler is encouraged to issue a warning.

title: C++ attribute: noreturn (since C++11)
summary: Indicates that the function does not return.

title: C++ attribute: optimize_for_synchronized (TM TS)
summary: Indicates that the function definition should be optimized for invocation from a synchronized statement.

title: Attribute specifier sequence (since C++11)
summary: Introduces implementation-defined attributes for types, objects, code, etc.

title: Placeholder type specifiers (since C++11)
summary: A placeholder type specifier designates a placeholder type that will be replaced later, typically by deduction from an initializer.

title: Basic concepts
summary: This section provides definitions for the specific terminology and the concepts used when describing the C++ programming language.

title: Bit-field
summary: Declares a class data member with explicit size, in bits. Adjacent bit-field members may (or may not) be packed to share and straddle the individual bytes.

title: Boolean literals
summary: 1 Syntax

title: break statement
summary: Causes the enclosing for, range-for, while or do-while loop or switch statement to terminate.

title: User-defined conversion function
summary: Enables implicit conversion or explicit conversion from a class type to another type.

title: Handling exceptions
summary: An exception can be handled by a handler.

title: Character literal
summary: 1 Syntax

title: Character sets and encodings
summary: This page describes several character sets specified by the C++ standard.

title: Class declaration
summary: Classes are user-defined types, defined by class-specifier, which appears in decl-specifier-seq of the declaration syntax.

title: Class template
summary: A class template defines a family of classes.

title: Class template argument deduction (CTAD) (since C++17)
summary: In order to instantiate a class template, every template argument must be known, but not every template argument has to be specified. In the following contexts the compiler will deduce the template arguments from the type of the initializer:

title: Classes
summary: A class is a user-defined type.

title: Conflicting declarations
summary: Unless otherwise specified, two declarations cannot (re)introduce the same entity. The program is ill-formed if such declarations exist.

title: const_cast conversion
summary: Converts between types with different cv-qualification.

title: Constant expressions
summary: Defines an expression that can be evaluated at compile time.

title: Constant initialization
summary: Sets the initial values of the static variables to a compile-time constant.

title: consteval specifier (since C++20)
summary: consteval - specifies that a function is an immediate function, that is, every call to the function must produce a compile-time constant

title: constexpr specifier (since C++11)
summary: constexpr - specifies that the value of a variable, structured binding(since C++26) or function can appear in constant expressions

title: constinit specifier (since C++20)
summary: constinit - asserts that a variable has static initialization, i.e. zero initialization and constant initialization, otherwise the program is ill-formed.

title: Constraints and concepts (since C++20)
summary: Class templates, function templates (include generic lambdas), and other templated functions (typically members of class templates) might be associated with a constraint, which specifies the requirements on template arguments, which can be used to select the most appropriate function overloads and template specializations.

title: continue statement
summary: Causes the remaining portion of the enclosing for, range-for, while or do-while loop body to be skipped.

title: Converting constructor
summary: A constructor that is not declared with the specifier explicit and which can be called with a single parameter(until C++11) is called a converting constructor.

title: Copy constructors
summary: A copy constructor is a constructor which can be called with an argument of the same class type and copies the content of the argument without mutating the argument.

title: Copy elision
summary: When certain criteria are met, the creation of a class object from a source object of the same type (ignoring cv-qualification) can be omitted, even if the selected constructor and/or the destructor for the object have side effects. This elision of object creation is called copy elision ﻿.

title: Copy-initialization
summary: Initializes an object from another object.

title: Coroutines (C++20)
summary: A coroutine is a function that can suspend execution to be resumed later. Coroutines are stackless: they suspend execution by returning to the caller, and the data that is required to resume execution is stored separately from the stack.

title: Curiously Recurring Template Pattern
summary: The Curiously Recurring Template Pattern is an idiom in which a class X derives from a class template Y, taking a template parameter Z, where Y is instantiated with Z = X. For example,

title: Class template argument deduction (CTAD) (since C++17)
summary: In order to instantiate a class template, every template argument must be known, but not every template argument has to be specified. In the following contexts the compiler will deduce the template arguments from the type of the initializer:

title: cv (const and volatile) type qualifiers
summary: Appear in any type specifier, including decl-specifier-seq of declaration grammar, to specify constness or volatility of the object being declared or of the type being named.

title: Non-static data members
summary: Non-static data members are declared in a member specification of a class.

title: Declarations
summary: Declarations are how names are introduced (or re-introduced) into the C++ program. Not all declarations actually declare anything, and each kind of entity is declared differently.

title: decltype specifier (since C++11)
summary: Inspects the declared type of an entity or the type and value category of an expression.

title: Class template argument deduction (CTAD) (since C++17)
summary: In order to instantiate a class template, every template argument must be known, but not every template argument has to be specified. In the following contexts the compiler will deduce the template arguments from the type of the initializer:

title: Default arguments
summary: Allows a function to be called without providing one or more trailing arguments.

title: Default comparisons (since C++20)
summary: Comparison operator functions can be explicitly defaulted to request the compiler to generate the corresponding default comparison for a class.

title: Default constructors
summary: A default constructor is a constructor which can be called with no arguments.

title: Default-initialization
summary: This is the initialization performed when an object is constructed with no initializer.

title: Definitions and ODR (One Definition Rule)
summary: Definitions are declarations that fully define the entity introduced by the declaration. Every declaration is a definition, except for the following:

title: delete expression
summary: Destroys object(s) previously allocated by the new-expression and releases obtained memory area.

title: Dependent names
summary: Inside the definition of a template (both class template and function template), the meaning of some constructs may differ from one instantiation to another. In particular, types and expressions may depend on types of type template parameters and values of non-type template parameters.

title: Derived classes
summary: Any class type (whether declared with class-key class or struct) may be declared as derived from one or more base classes which, in turn, may be derived from their own base classes, forming an inheritance hierarchy.

title: Destructors
summary: A destructor is a special member function that is called when the lifetime of an object ends. The purpose of the destructor is to free the resources that the object may have acquired during its lifetime.

title: Direct-initialization
summary: Initializes an object from explicit set of constructor arguments.

title: do-while loop
summary: Conditionally executes a statement repeatedly (at least once).

title: dynamic_cast conversion
summary: Safely converts pointers and references to classes up, down, and sideways along the inheritance hierarchy.

title: Empty base optimization
summary: Allows the size of an empty base subobject to be zero.

title: Elaborated type specifier
summary: Elaborated type specifiers may be used to refer to a previously-declared class name (class, struct, or union) or to a previously-declared enum name even if the name was hidden by a non-type declaration. They may also be used to declare new class names.

title: Enumeration declaration
summary: An enumeration is a distinct type whose value is restricted to a range of values (see below for details), which may include several explicitly named constants ("enumerators").

title: Escape sequences
summary: Escape sequences are used to represent certain special characters within string literals and character literals.

title: Order of evaluation
summary: Order of evaluation of any part of any expression, including order of evaluation of function arguments is unspecified (with some exceptions listed below). The compiler can evaluate operands and other subexpressions in any order, and may choose another order when the same expression is evaluated again.

title: Dynamic exception specification (until C++17)
summary: Lists the exceptions that a function might directly or indirectly throw.

title: Exceptions
summary: Exception handling provides a way of transferring control and information from some point in the execution of a program to a handler associated with a point previously passed by the execution (in other words, exception handling transfers control up the call stack).

title: explicit specifier
summary: 1 Syntax

title: Explicit type conversion
summary: Converts between types using a combination of explicit and implicit conversions.

title: Expressions
summary: An expression is a sequence of operators and their operands, that specifies a computation.

title: Extending the namespace std
summary: 1 Adding declarations to std

title: final specifier (since C++11)
summary: Specifies that a virtual function cannot be overridden in a derived class, or that a class cannot be derived from.

title: Floating-point literal
summary: Floating-point literal defines a compile-time constant whose value is specified in the source file.

title: Fold expressions (since C++17)
summary: Reduces (folds)) a pack over a binary operator.

title: for loop
summary: Conditionally executes a statement repeatedly, where the statement does not need to manage the loop condition.

title: Friend declaration
summary: The friend declaration appears in a class body and grants a function or another class access to private and protected members of the class where the friend declaration appears.

title: Function declaration
summary: A function declaration introduces the function name and its type. A function definition associates the function name/type with the function body.

title: Function template
summary: A function template defines a family of functions.

title: Functions
summary: Functions are C++ entities that associate a sequence of statements (a function body) with a name and a list of zero or more function parameters.

title: goto statement
summary: Transfers control unconditionally.

title: History of C++
summary: 1 Early C++

title: Identifiers
summary: An identifier is an arbitrarily long sequence of digits, underscores, lowercase and uppercase Latin letters, and most Unicode characters.

title: if statement
summary: Conditionally executes another statement.

title: Implicit conversions
summary: Implicit conversions are performed whenever an expression of some type T1 is used in context that does not accept that type, but accepts some other type T2; in particular:

title: Implicit conversions
summary: Implicit conversions are performed whenever an expression of some type T1 is used in context that does not accept that type, but accepts some other type T2; in particular:

title: Type
summary: Objects, references, functions including function template specializations, and expressions have a property called type, which both restricts the operations that are permitted for those entities and provides semantic meaning to the otherwise generic sequences of bits.

title: Initialization
summary: Initialization of a variable provides its initial value at the time of construction.

title: Constructors and member initializer lists
summary: Constructors are non-static member functions declared with a special declarator syntax, they are used to initialize objects of their class types.

title: Injected-class-name
summary: The injected-class-name is the unqualified name of a class within the scope of said class.

title: inline specifier
summary: The inline specifier, when used in a function's decl-specifier-seq, declares the function to be an inline function.

title: Integer literal
summary: Allows values of integer type to be used in expressions directly.

title: Lambda expressions (since C++11)
summary: Constructs a closure) (an unnamed function object capable of capturing variables in scope). Declarations _(static and const may be present, see below)_ _(since C++14) (generic lambda, static and const may be present, see below)_
signatures: ret operator()(params) { body }
template<template-params> ret operator()(params) { body }

title: Language linkage
summary: Provides for linkage between program units written in different programming languages.

title: Lifetime
summary: Every object and reference has a lifetime, which is a runtime property: for any object or reference, there is a point of execution of a program when its lifetime begins, and there is a moment when it ends.

title: List-initialization (since C++11)
summary: Initializes an object from a brace-enclosed initializer list.

title: Name lookup
summary: Name lookup is the procedure by which a name, when encountered in a program, is associated with the declaration that introduced it.

title: Main function
summary: A program shall contain a global function named main, which is the designated start of the program in hosted environment. It shall have one of the following forms:

title: Non-static member functions
summary: A non-static member function is a function that is declared in a member specification of a class without a static or friend specifier (see static member functions and friend declaration for the effect of those keywords).

title: Member templates
summary: Template declarations (class, function, and variables(since C++14)) can appear inside a member specification of any class, struct, or union that are not local classes.

title: Memory model
summary: Defines the semantics of computer memory storage for the purpose of the C++ abstract machine.

title: Modules (since C++20)
summary: Most C++ projects use multiple translation units, and so they need to share declarations and definitions across those units. The usage of headers is prominent for this purpose, an example being the standard library whose declarations can be provided by including the corresponding header.

title: Move assignment operator
summary: A move assignment operator is a non-template non-static member function with the name operator= that can be called with an argument of the same class type and copies the content of the argument, possibly mutating the argument.

title: Move constructors
summary: A move constructor is a constructor which can be called with an argument of the same class type and copies the content of the argument, possibly mutating the argument.

title: Move assignment operator
summary: A move assignment operator is a non-template non-static member function with the name operator= that can be called with an argument of the same class type and copies the content of the argument, possibly mutating the argument.

title: Multi-threaded executions and data races (since C++11)
summary: A thread of execution is a flow of control within a program that begins with the invocation of a specific top-level function (by std::thread, std::async, std::jthread(since C++20) or other means), and recursively including every function invocation subsequently executed by the thread.

title: Identifiers
summary: An identifier is an arbitrarily long sequence of digits, underscores, lowercase and uppercase Latin letters, and most Unicode characters.

title: Namespaces
summary: Namespaces provide a method for preventing name conflicts in large projects.

title: Namespace aliases
summary: Namespace aliases allow the programmer to define an alternate name for a namespace.

title: No Diagnostic Required
summary: "No diagnostic required" indicates that some phraseology is ill-formed according to the language rules, but a compiler need not issue any diagnostic or error message. Usually, the reason is that trying to detect these situations would result in prohibitively long compile times.

title: Nested classes
summary: A declaration of a class/struct or union may appear within another class. Such declaration declares a nested class.

title: Nested classes
summary: A declaration of a class/struct or union may appear within another class. Such declaration declares a nested class.

title: new expression
summary: Creates and initializes objects with dynamic storage duration, that is, objects whose lifetime is not necessarily limited by the scope in which they were created.

title: noexcept operator (since C++11)
summary: The noexcept operator performs a compile-time check that returns true if an expression is declared to not throw any exceptions.

title: noexcept specifier (since C++11)
summary: Specifies whether a function could throw exceptions.

title: nullptr, the pointer literal (since C++11)
summary: 1 Syntax

title: Object
summary: C++ programs create, destroy, refer to, access, and manipulate objects.

title: Object
summary: C++ programs create, destroy, refer to, access, and manipulate objects.

summary: C++ (and C) source code may be written in any non-ASCII 7-bit character set that includes the ISO 646:1983 invariant character set. However, several C++ operators and punctuators require characters that are outside of the ISO 646 codeset: {, }, [, ], #, \, ^, |, ~.

title: override specifier (since C++11)
summary: Specifies that a virtual function overrides another virtual function.

summary: Accesses the element of a pack at a specified index.

title: Partial template specialization
summary: Allows customizing class and variable(since C++14) templates for a given category of template arguments.

summary: "Pointer to implementation" or "pImpl" is a C++ programming technique that removes implementation details of a class from its object representation by placing them in a separate class, accessed through an opaque pointer:

title: Pointer declaration
summary: Declares a variable of a pointer or pointer-to-member type.

title: Punctuation
summary: These are the punctuation symbols in C++. The meaning of each symbol is detailed in the linked pages.

summary: A qualified name is a name that appears on the right hand side of the scope resolution operator :: (see also qualified identifiers). A qualified name may refer to a

title: RAII
summary: Resource Acquisition Is Initialization or RAII, is a C++ programming technique[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) which binds the life cycle of a resource that must be acquired before use (allocated heap memory, thread of execution, open socket, open file, locked mutex, disk space, database connection—anything that exists in limited supply) to the lifetime of an object.

title: Range-based for loop (since C++11)
summary: Executes a for loop over a range.

title: Reference declaration
summary: Declares a named variable as a reference, that is, an alias to an already-existing object or function.

title: Reference initialization
summary: Binds a reference to an object.

title: reinterpret_cast conversion
summary: Converts between types by reinterpreting the underlying bit pattern.

title: requires expression (since C++20)
summary: Yields a prvalue expression of type bool that describes the constraints.

title: return statement
summary: Terminates the current function and returns the specified value (if any) to the caller.

title: The rule of three/five/zero

title: Scope
summary: Each declaration that appears in a C++ program is only visible in some possibly discontiguous scopes.

title: SFINAE
summary: "Substitution Failure Is Not An Error"

title: sizeof... operator (since C++11)
summary: Queries the number of elements in a pack.

title: sizeof operator
summary: Queries size of the object or type.

title: Statements
summary: Statements are fragments of the C++ program that are executed in sequence. The body of any function is a sequence of statements.

title: static members
summary: Inside a class definition, the keyword static declares members that are not bound to class instances.

title: static_assert declaration (since C++11)
summary: Performs compile-time assertion checking.

title: static_cast conversion
summary: Converts between types using a combination of implicit and user-defined conversions.

title: Storage class specifiers
summary: The storage class specifiers are a part of the decl-specifier-seq of a name's declaration syntax. Together with the scope of the name, they control two independent properties of the name: its storage duration and its linkage.

summary: 1 Syntax

title: Structured binding declaration (since C++17)
summary: Binds the specified names to subobjects or elements of the initializer.

title: switch statement
summary: Transfers control to one of several statements, depending on the value of a condition.

title: Template argument deduction
summary: In order to instantiate a function template, every template argument must be known, but not every template argument has to be specified. When possible, the compiler will deduce the missing template arguments from the function arguments.

title: Template Metaprogramming
summary: Template metaprogramming is a family of techniques to create new types and compute values at compile time. C++ templates are Turing complete if there are no limits to the amount of recursive instantiations and the number of allowed state variables.

summary: C++ source files are processed by the compiler to produce C++ programs.

title: try block
summary: An exception thrown in a try block can possibly be handled by an associated handler.

title: Translation-unit-local entities (since C++20)
summary: Translation-unit-local (TU-local) entities are introduced to prevent entities that are supposed to be local (not used in any other translation unit) being exposed and used in other translation units.

title: Type
summary: Objects, references, functions including function template specializations, and expressions have a property called type, which both restricts the operations that are permitted for those entities and provides semantic meaning to the otherwise generic sequences of bits.

title: Type
summary: Objects, references, functions including function template specializations, and expressions have a property called type, which both restricts the operations that are permitted for those entities and provides semantic meaning to the otherwise generic sequences of bits.

title: Type alias, alias template (since C++11)
summary: Type alias is a name that refers to a previously defined type (similar to typedef).

title: typedef specifier
summary: typedef - creates an alias that can be used anywhere in place of a (possibly complex) type name.

title: typeid operator
summary: Queries information of a type.

title: Fundamental types
summary: (See also type for type system overview and the list of type-related utilities that are provided by the C++ library) Declarations _(since C++11)_
signatures: typedef decltype(nullptr) nullptr_t;

title: Undefined behavior
summary: Renders the entire program meaningless if certain rules of the language are violated.

title: Union declaration
summary: A union is a special class type that can hold only one of its non-static data members at a time.

title: Unqualified name lookup
summary: For an unqualified name, that is a name that does not appear to the right of a scope resolution operator ::, name lookup examines the scopes as described below, until it finds at least one declaration of any kind, at which time the lookup stops and no further scopes are examined. (Note: lookup from some contexts skips some declarations, for example, lookup of the name used to the left of :: ignores function, variable

title: User-defined literals (since C++11)
summary: Allows integer, floating-point, character, and string literals to produce objects of user-defined type by defining a user-defined suffix.

title: Using-declaration
summary: Introduces a name that is defined elsewhere into the declarative region where this using-declaration appears. See using enum and(since C++20)using namespace for other related declarations.

title: Usual arithmetic conversions
summary: Many binary operators that expect operands of arithmetic or enumeration type cause conversions and yield result types in a similar way. The purpose is to yield a common type, which is also the type of the result.

title: Value categories
summary: Each C++ expression (an operator with its operands, a literal, a variable name, etc.) is characterized by two independent properties: a type and a value category. Each expression has some non-reference type, and each expression belongs to exactly one of the three primary value categories: prvalue, xvalue, and lvalue.

title: Value-initialization
summary: This is the initialization performed when an object is constructed with an empty initializer.

title: Variable template (since C++14)
summary: A variable template defines a family of variables or static data members.

title: Variadic arguments
summary: Allows a function to accept any number of extra arguments.

title: virtual function specifier
summary: The virtual specifier specifies that a non-static member function is virtual and supports dynamic dispatch. It may only appear in the decl-specifier-seq of the initial declaration of a non-static member function (i.e., when it is declared in the class definition).

title: while loop
summary: Conditionally executes a statement repeatedly.

title: Zero-initialization
summary: Sets the initial value of an object to zero.

summary: The objective of this page is to build a comprehensive list of open-source C++ libraries, so that when one needs an implementation of particular functionality, one needn’t to waste time searching on web (DuckDuckGo, Google, Bing, etc.)

title: std::codecvt_utf8_utf16
summary: std::codecvt_utf8_utf16 is a std::codecvt facet which encapsulates conversion between a UTF-8 encoded byte string and UTF-16 encoded character string. If Elem is a 32-bit type, one UTF-16 code unit will be stored in each 32-bit character of the output sequence.
signatures: template< class Elem, unsigned long Maxcode = 0x10ffff, std::codecvt_mode Mode = (std::codecvt_mode)0 > class codecvt_utf8_utf16 : public std::codecvt<Elem, char, std::mbstate_t>;

This operator allows any locale object that has a collate facet to be used as a binary predicate in the standard algorithms (such as std::sort) and ordered containers (such as std::set).

title: Localization library
summary: The locale facility includes internationalization support for character classification and string collation, numeric, monetary, and date/time formatting and parsing, and message retrieval. Locale settings control the behavior of stream I/O, regular expression library, and other components of the C++ standard library.

title: Memory management library
summary: The C++ memory area spans ownership models, allocation strategies, polymorphic memory resources, raw storage algorithms, low-level pointer utilities, and the language-adjacent rules around object lifetime. This hub is the curated starting point when the problem is about how objects are stored, owned, created, destroyed, or allocated.

title: std::raw_storage_iterator
summary: The output iterator std::raw_storage_iterator makes it possible for standard algorithms to store results in uninitialized memory. Whenever the algorithm writes an object of type T to the dereferenced iterator, the object is copy-constructed into the location in the uninitialized storage pointed to by the iterator.

title: std::weak_ptr<T>::owner_hash
summary: Returns an unspecified value such that for any object other where owner_equal(other) is true, owner_hash() == other.owner_hash() is true. Declarations _(since C++26)_

title: std::weak_ptr<T>::reset
summary: Releases the reference to the managed object. After the call *this manages no object.

title: std::weak_ptr<T>::swap
summary: Exchanges the stored pointer values and the ownerships of *this and r. Reference counts, if any, are not adjusted.
signatures: void swap( weak_ptr& r ) noexcept;

title: std::swap(std::weak_ptr)
summary: Specializes the std::swap algorithm for std::weak_ptr. Swaps the contents of lhs and rhs.

title: std::weak_ptr<T>::use_count
summary: Returns the number of shared_ptr instances that share ownership of the managed object, or 0 if the managed object has already been deleted, i.e. *this is empty.
signatures: long use_count() const noexcept;

title: std::weak_ptr
summary: std::weak_ptr is a smart pointer that holds a non-owning ("weak") reference to an object that is managed by std::shared_ptr. It must be converted to std::shared_ptr in order to access the referenced object.
signatures: template< class T > class weak_ptr;

title: Metaprogramming library (since C++11)
summary: C++ provides metaprogramming facilities, such as type traits, compile-time rational arithmetic, and compile-time integer sequences.

title: C++ named requirements: AccessorPolicy (since C++23)
summary: AccessorPolicy defines how elements are accessed from a given data handle to such elements and an index. It is used as a policy in std::mdspan to specify the access to a reference of an element using an underlying 1D index.

title: C++ named requirements: Allocator
summary: Encapsulates strategies for access/addressing, allocation/deallocation and construction/destruction of objects. Declarations
signatures: template<class Alloc> concept /*simple-allocator*/ = requires(Alloc alloc, std::size_t n) { { *alloc.allocate(n) } -> std::same_as<typename Alloc::value_type&>; { alloc.deallocate(alloc.allocate(n), n) }; } && std::copy_constructible<Alloc> && std::equality_comparable<Alloc>;

title: C++ named requirements: AllocatorAwareContainer (since C++11)
summary: An AllocatorAwareContainer is a Container that holds an instance of an Allocator and uses that instance in all its member functions to allocate and deallocate memory and to construct and destroy objects in that memory (such objects may be container elements, nodes, or, for unordered containers, bucket arrays), except that std::basic_string specializations do not use the allocators for construction/destruction of thei

title: C++ named requirements: AssociativeContainer
summary: An AssociativeContainer is an ordered Container that provides fast lookup of objects based on keys.

title: C++ named requirements: BasicFormatter (since C++20)
summary: BasicFormatter is a type that abstracts formatting operations for a given formatting argument type and character type. Specializations of std::formatter are required to meet the requirements of BasicFormatter.

title: C++ named requirements: BasicLockable (since C++11)
summary: The BasicLockable requirements describe the minimal characteristics of types that provide exclusive blocking semantics for execution agents (i.e. threads).

title: C++ named requirements: LegacyBidirectionalIterator
summary: A LegacyBidirectionalIterator is a LegacyForwardIterator that can be moved in both directions (i.e. incremented and decremented).
signatures: template<class I> concept __LegacyBidirectionalIterator = __LegacyForwardIterator<I> && requires(I i) { { --i } -> std::same_as<I&>; { i-- } -> std::convertible_to<const I&>; { *i-- } -> std::same_as<std::iter_reference_t<I>>; };

title: C++ named requirements: BinaryPredicate
summary: BinaryPredicate is a set of requirements expected by some of the standard library facilities from the user-provided arguments.

title: C++ named requirements: BinaryTypeTrait (since C++11)
summary: A BinaryTypeTrait is a class template that describes a relationship between its two template type parameters with a std::integral_constant (typically std::bool_constant) specialization.

title: C++ named requirements: BitmaskType
summary: Defines a type that can be used to represent a set of constant values or any combination of those values. This trait is typically implemented by integer types, std::bitset, or enumerations (scoped and unscoped) with additional operator overloads.

title: C++ named requirements: BooleanTestable
summary: Specifies that an expression of such a type and value category is convertible to bool, and for which the logical operators for the type or two different BooleanTestable types have the usual behavior (including short-circuiting).

title: C++ named requirements: Callable
summary: A Callable type is a type for which the INVOKE and INVOKE<R> operations (used by, e.g., std::function, std::bind, and std::thread::thread) are applicable.

title: C++ named requirements: CharTraits
summary: CharTraits is a traits class that abstracts basic character and string operations for a given character type. Most standard library string and input/output classes require a CharTraits template type parameter alongside a corresponding character template type parameter.
signatures: template<> class char_traits<char>; template<> class char_traits<wchar_t>; template<> class char_traits<char8_t>; template<> class char_traits<char16_t>; template<> class char_traits<char32_t>;

title: C++ named requirements: Clock (since C++11)
summary: The Clock requirements describe a bundle consisting of a std::chrono::duration, a std::chrono::time_point, and a function now() to get the current time_point. The origin of the clock's time_point is referred to as the clock's epoch.

title: C++ named requirements: Compare
summary: Compare is a set of requirements expected by some of the standard library facilities from the user-provided function object types.

title: C++ named requirements: ConstexprIterator (since C++20)
summary: A ConstexprIterator is a LegacyIterator that can be used during constant expression evaluation.

title: C++ named requirements: Container
summary: A Container is an object used to store other objects and taking care of the management of the memory used by the objects it contains.

title: C++ named requirements: ContiguousContainer (since C++17)
summary: A ContiguousContainer is a Container that stores objects in contiguous memory locations.

title: C++ named requirements: LegacyContiguousIterator (since C++17)
summary: A LegacyContiguousIterator is a LegacyIterator whose logically adjacent elements are also physically adjacent in memory.

title: C++ named requirements: CopyAssignable
summary: Specifies that an instance of the type can be copy-assigned from an lvalue expression.

title: C++ named requirements: CopyConstructible
summary: Specifies that an instance of the type can be copy-constructed from an lvalue expression.

title: C++ named requirements: CopyInsertable (since C++11)
summary: Specifies that an object of the type can be copy-constructed in-place by a given allocator.

title: C++ named requirements: DefaultConstructible
summary: Specifies that an instance of the type can be default constructed.

title: C++ named requirements: DefaultInsertable (since C++11)
summary: Specifies that an object of the type can be default-constructed in-place by a given allocator.

title: C++ named requirements: Destructible
summary: Specifies that an instance of the type can be destructed.

title: C++ named requirements: EmplaceConstructible (since C++11)
summary: Specifies that an object of the type can be constructed from a given set of arguments in uninitialized storage by a given allocator.

title: C++ named requirements: EqualityComparable
summary: The type must work with == operator and the result should have standard semantics.

title: C++ named requirements: Erasable (since C++11)
summary: Specifies that an object of the type can be destroyed by a given Allocator.

title: C++ named requirements: FormattedInputFunction
summary: A FormattedInputFunction is a stream input function that performs the following:

title: C++ named requirements: FormattedOutputFunction
summary: 1 Requirements

title: C++ named requirements: Formatter (since C++20)
summary: Formatter is a type that abstracts formatting operations for a given formatting argument type and character type. Specializations of std::formatter provided by the standard library are required to meet the requirements of Formatter except as noted otherwise.

title: C++ named requirements: LegacyForwardIterator
summary: A LegacyForwardIterator is a LegacyIterator that can read data from the pointed-to element. Declarations
signatures: template<class It> concept __LegacyForwardIterator = __LegacyInputIterator<It> && std::constructible_from<It> && std::is_reference_v<std::iter_reference_t<It>> && std::same_as< std::remove_cvref_t<std::iter_reference_t<It>>, typename std::indirectly_readable_traits<It>::value_type> && requires(It it) { { it++ } -> std::convertible_to<const It&>; { *it++ } -> std::same_as<std::iter_reference_t<It>>; };

title: C++ named requirements: FunctionObject
summary: A FunctionObject type is the type of an object that can be used on the left of the function call operator.

title: C++ named requirements: Hash (since C++11)
summary: A Hash is a function object for which the output depends only on the input and has a very low probability of yielding the same output given different input values.

title: C++ named requirements: ImplicitLifetimeType
summary: Note: the standard doesn't define a named requirement with this name. This is a type category defined by the core language.

title: C++ named requirements: LegacyInputIterator
summary: A LegacyInputIterator is a LegacyIterator that can read from the pointed-to element. LegacyInputIterators only guarantee validity for single pass algorithms: once a LegacyInputIterator i has been incremented, all copies of its previous value may be invalidated.
signatures: template<class I> concept __LegacyInputIterator = __LegacyIterator<I> && std::equality_comparable<I> && requires(I i) { typename std::incrementable_traits<I>::difference_type; typename std::indirectly_readable_traits<I>::value_type; typename std::common_reference_t<std::iter_reference_t<I>&&, typename std::indirectly_readable_traits<I>::value_type&>; *i++; typename std::common_reference_t<decltype(*i++)&&, typename std::indirectly_readable_traits<I>::value_type&>; requires std::signed_integral<typename std::incrementable_traits<I>::difference_type>; };

title: C++ named requirements: LegacyIterator
summary: The LegacyIterator requirements describe types that can be used to identify and traverse the elements of a container. Declarations
signatures: template<class I> concept __LegacyIterator = requires(I i) { { *i } -> __Referenceable; { ++i } -> std::same_as<I&>; { *i++ } -> __Referenceable; } && std::copyable<I>;

title: C++ named requirements: LayoutMapping (since C++23)
summary: LayoutMapping controls the mapping of a multidimensional index to a one-dimensional offset to data handle in std::mdspan. Declarations _(exposition only*)_
signatures: template< class M > concept /*layout-mapping-alike*/ = requires { requires /*is-extents*/<typename M::extents_type>; { M::is_always_strided() } -> std::same_as<bool>; { M::is_always_exhaustive() } -> std::same_as<bool>; { M::is_always_unique() } -> std::same_as<bool>; std::bool_constant<M::is_always_strided()>::value; std::bool_constant<M::is_always_exhaustive()>::value; std::bool_constant<M::is_always_unique()>::value; };

title: C++ named requirements: LayoutMappingPolicy (since C++23)
summary: LayoutMappingPolicy is a type used to specify layout policy of std::mdspan. It provides a member alias template whose specialization is a LayoutMapping type.

title: C++ named requirements: LessThanComparable
summary: The type must work with < operator and the result should have standard semantics.

title: C++ named requirements: LiteralType (since C++11)
summary: Specifies that a type is a literal type. Literal types are the types of constexpr variables and they can be constructed, manipulated, and returned from constexpr functions.

title: C++ named requirements: Lockable (since C++11)
summary: The Lockable requirements extends the BasicLockable requirements to include attempted locking.

title: C++ named requirements: MoveAssignable (since C++11)
summary: Specifies that an instance of the type can be assigned from an rvalue argument.

title: C++ named requirements: MoveConstructible (since C++11)
summary: Specifies that an instance of the type can be constructed from an rvalue argument.

title: C++ named requirements: MoveInsertable (since C++11)
summary: Specifies that an object of the type can be constructed into uninitialized storage from an rvalue of that type by a given allocator.

title: C++ named requirements: Mutex (since C++11)
summary: The Mutex requirements extends the Lockable requirements to include inter-thread synchronization.

title: C++ named requirements: NullablePointer (since C++11)
summary: Specifies that the type is a pointer-like object which can be compared to std::nullptr_t objects.

title: C++ named requirements: NumericType
summary: Specifies that the type can be used as the template argument of std::valarray.

title: C++ named requirements: LegacyOutputIterator
summary: A LegacyOutputIterator is a LegacyIterator that can write to the pointed-to element.

title: C++ named requirements: PODType (deprecated in C++20)
summary: Specifies that the type is POD (Plain Old Data) type. This means the type is compatible with the types used in the C programming language, that is, can be exchanged with C libraries directly, in its binary form.

title: C++ named requirements: Predicate
summary: The Predicate requirements describe a callable that returns a BooleanTestable value.

title: C++ named requirements: LegacyRandomAccessIterator
summary: A LegacyRandomAccessIterator is a LegacyBidirectionalIterator that can be moved to point to any element in constant time. Declarations
signatures: template<class I> concept __LegacyRandomAccessIterator = __LegacyBidirectionalIterator<I> && std::totally_ordered<I> && requires(I i, typename std::incrementable_traits<I>::difference_type n) { { i += n } -> std::same_as<I&>; { i -= n } -> std::same_as<I&>; { i + n } -> std::same_as<I>; { n + i } -> std::same_as<I>; { i - n } -> std::same_as<I>; { i - i } -> std::same_as<decltype(n)>; { i[n] } -> std::convertible_to<std::iter_reference_t<I>>; };

title: C++ named requirements: RandomNumberDistribution (since C++11)
summary: A RandomNumberDistribution is a function object returning random numbers according to a probability density function p(x) or a discrete probability distribution P(xi).

title: C++ named requirements: RandomNumberEngine (since C++11)
summary: A random number engine is a function object returning unsigned integer values such that each value in the range of possible results has (ideally) equal probability.

title: C++ named requirements: RandomNumberEngineAdaptor (since C++11)
summary: A RandomNumberEngineAdaptor is a RandomNumberEngine that transforms the output of another RandomNumberEngine, the latter of which is termed a base engine.

title: C++ named requirements: RangeAdaptorClosureObject (since C++20)
summary: Range adaptor closure objects are FunctionObjects that are callable via the pipe operator: if C is a range adaptor closure object and R is a range, these two expressions are equivalent:

title: C++ named requirements: RangeAdaptorObject (since C++20)
summary: Range adaptor objects are customization point objects that accept viewable_range as their first arguments and return a view. Some range adaptor objects are unary, i.e.

title: C++ named requirements: RegexTraits (since C++11)
summary: RegexTraits is a traits class that abstracts operations needed for a given character type when as template type parameters for std::basic_regex. Declarations _(since C++11)(since C++11)_
signatures: template<> class regex_traits<char>; template<> class regex_traits<wchar_t>;

title: C++ named requirements: ReversibleContainer
summary: A ReversibleContainer is a Container that has iterators that meet the requirements of either LegacyBidirectionalIterator or LegacyRandomAccessIterator. Such iterators allow a ReversibleContainer to be iterated over in reverse.

title: C++ named requirements: ScalarType
summary: Note: the standard doesn't define a named requirement with this name. This is a type category defined by the core language.

title: C++ named requirements: SeedSequence (since C++11)
summary: A SeedSequence is an object that produces unsigned integer values i in the range 0 ≤ i < 232 based on a consumed range of integer data.

title: C++ named requirements: SequenceContainer
summary: A SequenceContainer is a Container that stores objects of the same type in a linear arrangement.

title: C++ named requirements: SharedLockable (since C++14)
summary: The SharedLockable requirements describe the minimal characteristics of types that provide shared blocking semantics for execution agents (i.e. threads).

title: C++ named requirements: SharedMutex (since C++17)
summary: The SharedMutex requirements extend the Mutex requirements to include shared lock ownership mode.

title: C++ named requirements: SharedTimedLockable (since C++14)
summary: The SharedTimedLockable requirements describe the characteristics of types that provide timed shared blocking semantics for execution agents (threads, processes, tasks).

title: C++ named requirements: SharedTimedMutex (since C++14)
summary: The SharedTimedMutex requirements extend the TimedMutex requirements to include shared lock ownership mode.

title: C++ named requirements: StandardLayoutType (since C++11)
summary: Specifies that a type is a standard layout type. Standard layout types are useful for communicating with code written in other programming languages.

title: C++ named requirements: Swappable
summary: Any lvalue or rvalue of this type can be swapped with any lvalue or rvalue of some other type, using unqualified function call swap() in the context where both std::swap and the user-defined swap()s are visible.

title: C++ named requirements: TimedLockable (since C++11)
summary: The TimedLockable requirements describe the characteristics of types that provide timed exclusive blocking semantics for execution agents (threads, processes, tasks).

title: C++ named requirements: TimedMutex (since C++11)
summary: The TimedMutex requirements extend the TimedLockable requirements to include inter-thread synchronization.

title: C++ named requirements: TransformationTrait (since C++11)
summary: A TransformationTrait is a class template that provides a transformation of its template type parameter.

title: C++ named requirements: TrivialClock (since C++11)
summary: The TrivialClock requirements describe the requirements satisfied by several clocks in the chrono library.

title: C++ named requirements: TrivialType (since C++11)(deprecated in C++26)
summary: Specifies that a type is a trivial type.

title: C++ named requirements: TriviallyCopyable (since C++11)
summary: Note: the standard doesn't define a named requirement with this name. This is a type category defined by the core language.

title: C++ named requirements: UnaryTypeTrait (since C++11)
summary: A UnaryTypeTrait is a class template that describes a property of its template type parameter with an std::integral_constant (typically std::bool_constant) specialization.

title: C++ named requirements: UnformattedInputFunction
summary: An UnformattedInputFunction is a stream input function that performs the following:

title: C++ named requirements: UnformattedOutputFunction
summary: A UnformattedOutputFunction is a stream output function that performs the following:

title: C++ named requirements: UniformRandomBitGenerator (since C++11)
summary: A uniform random bit generator is a function object returning unsigned integer values such that each value in the range of possible results has (ideally) equal probability.

title: C++ named requirements: UnorderedAssociativeContainer (since C++11)
summary: Unordered associative containers are Containers that provide fast lookup of objects based on keys. Worst case complexity is linear but on average much faster for most of the operations.

title: C++ named requirements: ValueSwappable (since C++11)
summary: Two objects of this type can be dereferenced and the resulting values can be swapped using unqualified function call swap() in the context where both std::swap and the user-defined swap()s are visible.

title: Named Requirements
summary: The named requirements listed on this page are the named requirements used in the normative text of the C++ standard to define the expectations of the standard library.

title: Numerics library
summary: The C++ numerics area combines several different problem spaces: scalar arithmetic helpers, bit-level integer tools, mathematical functions, random engines and distributions, complex numbers, compile-time ratios, floating-point control, and newer data-parallel facilities. This hub is the curated entry point when you know the task is numeric, but not yet which numeric model or header family you need.

title: std::add_sat
summary: Computes the saturating addition x + y. This operation (unlike built-in arithmetic operations on integers) behaves as-if it is a mathematical operation with an infinite range.
signatures: template< class T > constexpr T add_sat( T x, T y ) noexcept;

title: std::bit_cast
summary: Obtain a value of type To by reinterpreting the object representation of From. Every bit in the value representation of the returned To object is equal to the corresponding bit in the object representation of from.
signatures: template< class To, class From > constexpr To bit_cast( const From& from ) noexcept;

title: std::bit_ceil
summary: Calculates the smallest integral power of two that is not smaller than x. Declarations _(since C++20)_
signatures: template< class T > constexpr T bit_ceil( T x );

title: std::bit_floor
summary: If x is not zero, calculates the largest integral power of two that is not greater than x. If x is zero, returns zero.
signatures: template< class T > constexpr T bit_floor( T x ) noexcept;

title: std::bit_width
summary: If x is not zero, calculates the number of bits needed to store the value x, that is, \(1 + \lfloor \log_2(x) \rfloor\)1 + floor(log2(x)). If x is zero, returns zero.
signatures: template< class T > constexpr int bit_width( T x ) noexcept;

title: std::byteswap
summary: Reverses the bytes in the given integer value n. Declarations _(since C++23)_
signatures: template< class T > constexpr T byteswap( T n ) noexcept;

title: std::div_sat
summary: Computes the saturating division x / y. If T is a signed integer type, x is the smallest (most negative) value of T, and y == -1, returns the greatest value of T; otherwise, returns x / y.
signatures: template< class T > constexpr T div_sat( T x, T y ) noexcept;

title: FE_DFL_ENV
summary: The macro constant FE_DFL_ENV expands to an expression of type const std::fenv_t*, which points to a full copy of the default floating-point environment, that is, the environment as loaded at program startup. Declarations _(since C++11)_
signatures: #define FE_DFL_ENV /*implementation defined*/

title: FE_DIVBYZERO, FE_INEXACT, FE_INVALID, FE_OVERFLOW, FE_UNDERFLOW, FE_ALL_EXCEPT
summary: All these macro constants (except FE_ALL_EXCEPT) expand to integer constant expressions that are distinct powers of 2, which uniquely identify all supported floating-point exceptions. Each macro is defined only if it is supported.
signatures: #define FE_DIVBYZERO /*implementation defined power of 2*/
#define FE_INEXACT /*implementation defined power of 2*/
#define FE_INVALID /*implementation defined power of 2*/
#define FE_OVERFLOW /*implementation defined power of 2*/
#define FE_UNDERFLOW /*implementation defined power of 2*/
#define FE_ALL_EXCEPT FE_DIVBYZERO | FE_INEXACT | \ FE_INVALID | FE_OVERFLOW | \ FE_UNDERFLOW

title: FE_DOWNWARD, FE_TONEAREST, FE_TOWARDZERO, FE_UPWARD
summary: Each of these macro constants expands to a nonnegative integer constant expression, which can be used with std::fesetround and std::fegetround to indicate one of the supported floating-point rounding modes. The implementation may define additional rounding mode constants in <cfenv>, which should all begin with FE_ followed by at least one uppercase letter.
signatures: #define FE_DOWNWARD /*implementation defined*/
#define FE_TONEAREST /*implementation defined*/
#define FE_TOWARDZERO /*implementation defined*/
#define FE_UPWARD /*implementation defined*/

title: Floating-point environment (since C++11)
summary: The floating-point environment is the set of floating-point status flags and control modes supported by the implementation. It is thread-local.

title: std::feclearexcept
summary: Attempts to clear the floating-point exceptions that are listed in the bitmask argument excepts, which is a bitwise OR of the floating point exception macros. Declarations _(since C++11)_
signatures: int feclearexcept( int excepts );

title: std::fegetenv, std::fesetenv
summary: Manages the status of the floating-point environment. Declarations _(since C++11)_ _(since C++11)_
signatures: int fegetenv( std::fenv_t* envp )
int fesetenv( const std::fenv_t* envp );

title: std::fegetexceptflag, std::fesetexceptflag
summary: 1) Attempts to obtain the full contents of the floating-point exception flags that are listed in the bitmask argument excepts, which is a bitwise OR of the floating point exception macros. Declarations _(since C++11)_ _(since C++11)_
signatures: int fegetexceptflag( std::fexcept_t* flagp, int excepts );
int fesetexceptflag( const std::fexcept_t* flagp, int excepts );

title: std::feholdexcept
summary: First, saves the current floating-point environment to the object pointed to by envp (similar to std::fegetenv), then clears all floating-point status flags, and then installs the non-stop mode: future floating-point exceptions will not interrupt execution (will not trap), until the floating-point environment is restored by std::feupdateenv or std::fesetenv. Declarations _(since C++11)_
signatures: int feholdexcept( std::fenv_t* envp )

title: std::feraiseexcept
summary: Attempts to raise all floating point exceptions listed in excepts (a bitwise OR of the floating point exception macros). If one of the exceptions is FE_OVERFLOW or FE_UNDERFLOW, this function may additionally raise FE_INEXACT.
signatures: int feraiseexcept( int excepts );

title: std::fegetround, std::fesetround
summary: Manages the floating-point rounding direction. Declarations _(since C++11)_ _(since C++11)_
signatures: int fesetround( int round )
int fegetround()

title: std::fetestexcept
summary: Determines which of the specified subset of the floating point exceptions are currently set. The argument excepts is a bitwise OR of the floating point exception macros.
signatures: int fetestexcept( int excepts );

title: std::feupdateenv
summary: First, remembers the currently raised floating-point exceptions, then restores the floating-point environment from the object pointed to by envp (similar to std::fesetenv), then raises the floating-point exceptions that were saved. Declarations _(since C++11)_
signatures: int feupdateenv( const std::fenv_t* envp )

title: Floating-point environment (since C++11)

title: std::uniform_random_bit_generator
summary: The concept uniform_random_bit_generator<G> specifies that G is the type of a uniform random bit generator, that is, objects of type G is a function object returning unsigned integer values such that each value in the range of possible results has (ideally) equal probability of being returned. Declarations _(since C++20)_
signatures: template< class G > concept uniform_random_bit_generator = std::invocable<G&> && std::unsigned_integral<std::invoke_result_t<G&>> && requires { { G::min() } -> std::same_as<std::invoke_result_t<G&>>; { G::max() } -> std::same_as<std::invoke_result_t<G&>>; requires std::bool_constant<(G::min() < G::max())>::value; };

title: std::sub_sat

signatures: template< class T > constexpr T sub_sat( T x, T y ) noexcept;

title: std::valarray
summary: std::valarray is the class for representing and manipulating arrays of values. It supports element-wise mathematical operations and various forms of generalized subscript operators, slicing and indirect access.

Objects of type std::gslice can be used as indices with valarray's operator[] to select, for example, columns of a multidimensional array represented as a valarray.

title: Conditional inclusion
summary: The preprocessor supports conditional compilation of parts of source file. This behavior is controlled by #if, #else, #elif, #ifdef, #ifndef, #elifdef, #elifndef(since C++23), and #endif directives.

title: Diagnostic directives
summary: Shows the given error message and renders the program ill-formed, or shows the given warning message without affecting the validity of the program(since C++23).

title: Implementation defined behavior control
summary: Implementation defined behavior is controlled by #pragma directive.

title: Source file inclusion
summary: Includes other source file into current source file at the line immediately after the directive.

title: Filename and line information
summary: Changes the source code's line number and, optionally, the current file name, in the preprocessor.

title: Replacing text macros
summary: The preprocessor supports text macro replacement. Function-like text macro replacement is also supported.

title: Preprocessor
summary: The preprocessor is executed at translation phase 4, before the compilation. The result of preprocessing is a single file which is then passed to the actual compiler.

title: std::ranges::views::chunk_by, std::ranges::chunk_by_view
summary: 1) chunk_by_view is a range adaptor that takes a view and an invocable object pred (the binary predicate), and produces a view of subranges (chunks), by splitting the underlying view between each pair of adjacent elements for which pred returns false. The first element of each such pair belongs to the previous chunk, and the second element belongs to the next chunk.

title: std::ranges::views::concat, std::ranges::concat_view
summary: concat_view presents a view factory that takes an arbitrary number of ranges as an argument list, and provides a view that starts at the first element of the first range, ends at the last element of the last range, with all range elements sequenced in between respectively in the order given in the arguments, effectively concatenating, or chaining together the argument ranges. Declarations _(since C++26)_ _(since C++2
signatures: template< ranges::input_range... Views > requires (ranges::view<Views> && ...) && (sizeof...(Views) > 0) && /*concatable*/<Views...> class concat_view : public ranges::view_interface<concat_view<Views...>>
namespace views { inline constexpr /* unspecified */ concat = /* unspecified */; }
Call signature
template< ranges::viewable_range... Rs > requires /* see below */ constexpr ranges::view auto concat( Rs&&... rs );
Helper type aliases
template< class... Rs > using /*concat-reference-t*/ = ranges::common_reference_t<ranges::range_reference_t<Rs>...>;

title: Ranges library (since C++20)

title: Regular expressions library
summary: The C++ regular expressions library is built around a small set of cooperating pieces: a compiled regex object, algorithms that apply it to character ranges, result objects that describe matches, iterators that walk repeated matches or tokens, and option/flag types that change both syntax and matching behavior. This hub helps you choose the right regex surface before you dive into the lower-level details.

title: C++ Standard Library
summary: The C++ standard library provides the reusable building blocks of the language: containers, algorithms, numerics, strings, I/O, concurrency, utilities, and the language support facilities that make the rest of the library work. <div class="overview-note"> This page is an overview hub, not just a raw converted reference leaf.

title: std::strxfrm

title: std::tolower
summary: Converts the given character to lowercase according to the character conversion rules defined by the currently installed C locale. Declarations
signatures: int tolower( int ch );

title: std::toupper
summary: Converts the given character to uppercase according to the character conversion rules defined by the currently installed C locale. Declarations
signatures: int toupper( int ch );

title: Null-terminated byte strings
summary: A null-terminated byte string (NTBS) is a possibly empty sequence of nonzero bytes followed by a byte with value zero (the terminating null character). Each byte in a byte string encodes one character of some character set.

title: std::char_traits
summary: The char_traits class is a traits class template that abstracts basic character and string operations for a given character type. The defined operation set is such that generic algorithms almost always can be implemented in terms of it.
signatures: template< class CharT > class char_traits;

title: std::mbsinit

signatures: int mbsinit( const std::mbstate_t* ps);

title: std::mbsrtowcs
summary: Converts a null-terminated multibyte character sequence, which begins in the conversion state described by *ps, from the array whose first element is pointed to by *src to its wide character representation. If dst is not null, converted characters are stored in the successive elements of the wchar_t array pointed to by dst.
signatures: std::size_t mbsrtowcs( wchar_t* dst, const char** src, std::size_t len, std::mbstate_t* ps );

title: std::mbstate_t
summary: The type mbstate_t is a trivial non-array type that can represent any of the conversion states that can occur in an implementation-defined set of supported multibyte character encoding rules. Zero-initialized value of mbstate_t represents the initial conversion state, although other values of mbstate_t may exist that also represent the initial conversion state.
signatures: struct mbstate_t;

title: std::mbstowcs
summary: Converts a multibyte character string from the array whose first element is pointed to by src to its wide character representation. Converted characters are stored in the successive elements of the array pointed to by dst.
signatures: std::size_t mbstowcs( wchar_t* dst, const char* src, std::size_t len );

title: std::mbtowc
summary: Converts a multibyte character whose first byte is pointed to by s to a wide character, written to *pwc if pwc is not null. Declarations
signatures: int mbtowc( wchar_t* pwc, const char* s, std::size_t n );

title: std::wcrtomb
summary: Converts a wide character to its narrow multibyte representation. Declarations
signatures: std::size_t wcrtomb( char* s, wchar_t wc, std::mbstate_t* ps );

title: std::wcsrtombs
summary: Converts a sequence of wide characters from the array whose first element is pointed to by *src to its narrow multibyte representation that begins in the conversion state described by *ps. If dst is not null, converted characters are stored in the successive elements of the char array pointed to by dst.
signatures: std::size_t wcsrtombs( char* dst, const wchar_t** src, std::size_t len, std::mbstate_t* ps );

title: std::wcstombs
summary: Converts a sequence of wide characters from the array whose first element is pointed to by src to its narrow multibyte representation that begins in the initial shift state. Converted characters are stored in the successive elements of the char array pointed to by dst.
signatures: std::size_t wcstombs( char* dst, const wchar_t* src, std::size_t len );

title: std::wctob

signatures: int wctob( std::wint_t c );

title: std::wctomb
summary: Converts a wide character wc to multibyte encoding and stores it (including any shift sequences) in the char array whose first element is pointed to by s. No more than MB_CUR_MAX characters are stored.
signatures: int wctomb( char* s, wchar_t wc );

title: Null-terminated multibyte strings
summary: A null-terminated multibyte string (NTMBS), or "multibyte string", is a sequence of nonzero bytes followed by a byte with value zero (the terminating null character).

title: Null-terminated wide strings
summary: A null-terminated wide string is a sequence of valid wide characters, ending with a null character.

title: std::iswalnum
summary: Checks if the given wide character is an alphanumeric character, i.e. either a number (0123456789), an uppercase letter (ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ), a lowercase letter (abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz) or any alphanumeric character specific to the current locale.
signatures: int iswalnum( std::wint_t ch );

title: std::iswalpha
summary: Checks if the given wide character is an alphabetic character, i.e. either an uppercase letter (ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ), a lowercase letter (abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz) or any alphabetic character specific to the current locale.
signatures: int iswalpha( std::wint_t ch );

title: std::iswblank
summary: Checks if the given wide character is classified as blank character (that is, a whitespace character used to separate words in a sentence) by the current C locale. In the default C locale, only space (0x20) and horizontal tab (0x09) are blank characters.
signatures: int iswblank( std::wint_t ch );

title: std::iswcntrl
summary: Checks if the given wide character is a control character, i.e. codes 0x00-0x1F and 0x7F and any control characters specific to the current locale.
signatures: int iswcntrl( std::wint_t ch );

title: std::iswctype
summary: Classifies the wide character wc using the current C locale's LC_CTYPE category identified by desc. Declarations
signatures: int iswctype( std::wint_t wc, std::wctype_t desc );

title: std::iswdigit
summary: Checks if the given wide character corresponds (if narrowed) to one of the ten decimal digit characters 0123456789. Declarations
signatures: int iswdigit( wint_t ch );

title: std::iswgraph
summary: Checks if the given wide character has a graphical representation, i.e. it is either a number (0123456789), an uppercase letter (ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ), a lowercase letter (abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz), a punctuation character (!"#$%&'()*+,-./:;<=>?@[\]^_`{|}~) or any graphical character specific to the current C locale.
signatures: int iswgraph( std::wint_t ch );

title: std::iswlower
summary: Checks if the given wide character is a lowercase letter, i.e. one of abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz or any lowercase letter specific to the current locale.
signatures: int iswlower( std::wint_t ch );

title: std::iswprint
summary: Checks if the given wide character can be printed, i.e. it is either a number (0123456789), an uppercase letter (ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ), a lowercase letter (abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz), a punctuation character (!"#$%&'()*+,-./:;<=>?@[\]^_`{!}~), space or any printable character specific to the current C locale.
signatures: int iswprint( std::wint_t ch );

title: std::iswpunct
summary: Checks if the given wide character is a punctuation character, i.e. it is one of !"#$%&'()*+,-./:;<=>?@[\]^_`{|}~ or any punctuation character specific to the current locale.
signatures: int iswpunct( std::wint_t ch );

title: std::iswspace
summary: Checks if the given wide character is a wide whitespace character as classified by the currently installed C locale. In the default locale, the whitespace characters are the following: Declarations
signatures: int iswspace( wint_t ch );

title: std::iswupper
summary: Checks if the given wide character is an uppercase letter, i.e. one of ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ or any uppercase letter specific to the current locale.
signatures: int iswupper( std::wint_t ch );

title: std::iswxdigit
summary: Checks if the given wide character corresponds (if narrowed) to a hexadecimal numeric character, i.e. one of 0123456789abcdefABCDEF.
signatures: int iswxdigit( wint_t ch );

title: std::towctrans
summary: Maps the wide character ch using the current C locale's LC_CTYPE mapping category identified by desc. Declarations
signatures: std::wint_t towctrans( std::wint_t ch, std::wctrans_t desc );

title: std::towlower
summary: Converts the given wide character to lowercase, if possible. Declarations
signatures: std::wint_t towlower( std::wint_t ch );

title: std::towupper
summary: Converts the given wide character to uppercase, if possible. Declarations
signatures: std::wint_t towupper( std::wint_t ch );

wchar_t* wcschr( wchar_t* str, wchar_t ch );

signatures: int wcscmp( const wchar_t* lhs, const wchar_t* rhs );

title: std::wcscoll
summary: Compares two null-terminated wide strings according to the locale most recently installed by std::setlocale, as defined by the LC_COLLATE category. Declarations

summary: Copies the wide string pointed to by src (including the terminating null wide character) to wide character array pointed to by dest. Declarations

summary: Returns the length of the maximum initial segment of the wide string pointed to by dest, that consists of only the characters not found in wide string pointed to by src. Declarations

summary: Returns the length of a wide string, that is the number of non-null wide characters that precede the terminating null wide character. Declarations
signatures: std::size_t wcslen( const wchar_t* str );

wchar_t* wcspbrk( wchar_t* dest, const wchar_t* src );

title: Text processing library
summary: The C++ text area is intentionally narrow today. Its core standard-library surface is std::text_encoding, which gives programs a vocabulary for talking about ordinary, wide, literal, and environment encodings.

title: std::text_encoding
summary: The class text_encoding provides a mechanism for identifying character encodings. It is used to determine the ordinary character literal encoding of the translation environment at compile-time and the character encoding of the execution environment at runtime.
signatures: struct text_encoding;

title: Thread support library
summary: The C++ thread support library covers several concurrency models at once: raw threads and joining, mutex and lock orchestration, waiting and notification, task/future result transport, cooperative cancellation, and newer coordination primitives such as latches, barriers, and semaphores. This hub helps you choose the right concurrency surface before you drop into specific classes or member functions.

title: std::async
summary: The function template std::async runs the function f asynchronously (potentially in a separate thread which might be a part of a thread pool) and returns a std::future that will eventually hold the result of that function call. Declarations _(since C++11)_ _(since C++11)_
signatures: template< class F, class... Args > std::future</* see below */> async( F&& f, Args&&... args );
template< class F, class... Args > std::future</* see below */> async( std::launch policy, F&& f, Args&&... args );

title: std::barrier
summary: The class template std::barrier provides a thread-coordination mechanism that blocks a group of threads of known size until all threads in that group have reached the barrier. Unlike std::latch, barriers are reusable: once a group of arriving threads are unblocked, the barrier can be reused.
signatures: template< class CompletionFunction = /* see below */ > class barrier;

title: std::barrier<CompletionFunction>::arrive
summary: Constructs an arrival_token object associated with the phase synchronization point for the current phase. Then, decrements the expected count by n.
signatures: arrival_token arrive( std::ptrdiff_t n = 1 );

title: std::barrier<CompletionFunction>::arrive_and_drop
summary: Decrements the initial expected count for all subsequent phases by one, and then decrements the expected count for the current phase by one. Declarations _(since C++20)_
signatures: void arrive_and_drop();

title: std::barrier<CompletionFunction>::arrive_and_wait
summary: Atomically decrements the expected count by 1, then blocks at the synchronization point for the current phase until the phase completion step of the current phase is run. Equivalent to wait(arrive());.
signatures: void arrive_and_wait();

title: std::barrier<CompletionFunction>::barrier
summary: 1) Sets the both initial expected count for each phase and the current expected count for the first phase to expected, initializes the completion function object with std::move(f), and then starts the first phase. The behavior is undefined if expected is negative or greater than max().
signatures: constexpr explicit barrier( std::ptrdiff_t expected, CompletionFunction f = CompletionFunction());
barrier( const barrier& ) = delete;

title: std::barrier<CompletionFunction>::max
summary: Returns the maximum value of the expected count supported by the implementation. Declarations _(since C++20)_
signatures: static constexpr std::ptrdiff_t max() noexcept;

title: std::barrier<CompletionFunction>::wait
summary: If arrival is associated with the phase synchronization point for the current phase of *this, blocks at the synchronization point associated with arrival until the phase completion step of the synchronization point's phase is run. Declarations _(since C++20)_
signatures: void wait( arrival_token&& arrival ) const;

title: std::barrier<CompletionFunction>::~barrier
summary: Destroys the barrier. Declarations _(since C++20)_
signatures: ~barrier();
